coredata.dc:title: Synthesized novel carbon dots as green corrosion inhibitor for mild steel in hydrochloric acid: Gravimetric, electrochemical and morphological studies
coredata.dc:creator: Mehta, Raj Kumar
Gupta, Sujata Kumari
Yadav, Mahendra
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), mainly heteroatom-doped CDs, have been recognized as efficient eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors due to their low cost, excellent chemical stability, good water solubility, and nontoxic properties. Inspired by this, in present investigation a pair of nitrogen-doped eco-friendly CDs (CD1 and CD2) have been synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal technique, and their anticorrosion performance for the mild steel (MS) in 15 % HCl solution has been studied. The synthesized CD1 and CD2 have been characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy, photoluminescence (PL), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The anticorrosion proficiency of the CD1 and CD2 have been studied using weight-loss, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic studies and it was found that obtained results are in agreement with each other at all concentrations. Corrosion inhibition efficiency of both inhibitors increases with increasing concentration and reaching 96.8 % (CD1) and 94.7 % (CD2) after 6 h immersion at 150 ppm concentration. The adsorption of inhibitors CD1 and CD2 on MS obeyed Langmuir adsorption isotherm and physical adsorption process. Potentiodynamic polarization study suggested that CD1 and CD2 act as mixed type inhibitor and alter the rates of anodic dissolution of MS and cathodic hydrogen evaluation reaction simultaneously. The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) and XPS studies confirmed the formation of protective film of inhibitor molecules at the MS surface.

head.ce:title: Synthesized novel carbon dots as green corrosion inhibitor for mild steel in hydrochloric acid: Gravimetric, electrochemical and morphological studies

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Biology, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad 826004, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Biology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Chemical Biology, Indian Institute of Technology (ISM), Dhanbad-826004, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), mainly heteroatom-doped CDs, have been recognized as efficient eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors due to their low cost, excellent chemical stability, good water solubility, and nontoxic properties. Inspired by this, in present investigation a pair of nitrogen-doped eco-friendly CDs (CD1 and CD2) have been synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal technique, and their anticorrosion performance for the mild steel (MS) in 15 % HCl solution has been studied. The synthesized CD1 and CD2 have been characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy, photoluminescence (PL), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The anticorrosion proficiency of the CD1 and CD2 have been studied using weight-loss, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic studies and it was found that obtained results are in agreement with each other at all concentrations. Corrosion inhibition efficiency of both inhibitors increases with increasing concentration and reaching 96.8 % (CD1) and 94.7 % (CD2) after 6 h immersion at 150 ppm concentration. The adsorption of inhibitors CD1 and CD2 on MS obeyed Langmuir adsorption isotherm and physical adsorption process. Potentiodynamic polarization study suggested that CD1 and CD2 act as mixed type inhibitor and alter the rates of anodic dissolution of MS and cathodic hydrogen evaluation reaction simultaneously. The field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) and XPS studies confirmed the formation of protective film of inhibitor molecules at the MS surface.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to excellent mechanical properties and low cost, mild steel (MS) occupies a significant status in the structural materials and has become the first choice for the engineering applications [1]. So, it is widely used in daily life as well as in variety of industries [2]. But unfortunately, it is easily corroded and its corrosion rate is accelerated, when mineral acid solutions are being used in acid treatment to eliminate undesirable rusts and scales in the industrial sectors [3]. Corrosion is a natural and spontaneous destruction of metals by physical, chemical and biological interaction with the environment in which it is placed [4]. Metallic corrosion is very dangerous and its impact is very harmful to human life and environment. It causes structural damage or failure of metallic equipment and loss of billions of dollars. India annually losses approximately 4 % of its annual gross domestic product (GDP), while US losses 3.4 % of GDP as reported by national association of corrosion engineers (NACE) [5]. Despite a continuous commitment on research and innovation to prevent corrosion, it remains a key challenge for researchers. Corrosion adversely affect the chemical and petroleum industries. Silicates and carbonates deposits in petroleum oil wells during the mining of crude product from subterranean mines restrict the flow of petroleum product and reduce the production [6]. So, in order to enhance the production and reopen the flow channels, acid treatment with 15–28 % acid solution is used [7]. It has been reported that typically 15 % HCl solution is the prime choice for acid treatment because it reacts with silicates, carbonates and other form of scale to form water soluble compounds [8]. The application of acid solution cause corrosion of metal in its contact therefore to suppress the corrosion, inhibitors are mixed in the acid solution before the acid treatment. Arsenate, vanadate, chromate, etc. are well recognized inorganic inhibitors but their toxic nature limits their application [9]. Researchers reported that heterocyclic compounds containing nitrogen, sulfur and oxygen act as good corrosion inhibitor but some of them are also toxic and most of them are insoluble in aqueous acidic solution which limits their application as useful corrosion inhibitor [10]. In literature, some ionic liquids [11–14], extracts of plants [15–18] and carbon dots [19,20] have been reported as efficient eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors. Out of these inhibitors, carbon dots (CDs) are very interesting because they are water soluble, nontoxic, good biocompatible and safe to use. So, at present researchers focused on the synthesis and application of CDs as effective and green corrosion inhibitor. Carbon related materials and CDs are reported as good corrosion inhibitor [21–23] as well as good supercapacitor [24–28].

The objective of the present investigation is to synthesise two CDs, namely, CD1 and CD2 by using glucose, arginine and histadine as starting materials and to investigate the corrosion inhibition efficiency for the MS in 15 % HCl solution. Photoluminescence, UV–vis, HRTEM, XPS, and FTIR studies were performed to characterize the synthesized inhibitors. The corrosion inhibition proficiencies of both CDs were measured through weight-loss, potentiodynamic polarization (PP), and EIS studies. For surface studies FESEM, AFM, and XPS were executed. As per literature survey, corrosion inhibition efficiency of CD1 and CD2 on mild steel in 15 % HCl solution is not reported. In the present study we have investigated novel efficient eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors (CD1 and CD2) which are the current need of industries. We have used most of the advanced characterization techniques and studies related with corrosion inhibition studies. The reported inhibitors have potential industrial application.

The synthesis of CD1 and CD2 was done by previously reported hydrothermal method with slight modification [29]. Briefly, for the synthesis of CD1, an equimolar mixture of glucose and arginine was dissolved in 30 mL double distilled water and sonicated for 60 s. for complete dissolution. The resulting solution was carefully poured into an autoclave and placed in an oven for 8 h at 180 °C. After the completion of reaction, the autoclave was cooled at room temperature and the product was filter and centrifuged to remove macro-particles. The filtrate solution exhibited blue fluorescent colour in UV light. Then, the filtrate solution was dried and kept in desiccator for corrosion testing. The same procedure was repeated for the synthesis of CD2 corrosion inhibitor but histidine was used in place of arginine. The synthetic path for the synthesis of CD1 & CD2 inhibitors is presented in Fig. 1 .

A thermo scientific 200 kV HRTEM: model F200X G2 was used to record shape and diameter of nano-particles. The functional groups of CD1 and CD2 were recorded by FTIR-instrument, model: spectrum 2000, made by Perkin Elmer, USA. The conjugation and fluorescent properties were measured using an Epoch 2 microplate reader (UV-VIS spectroscopy) and a Hitachi F-7000 photoluminescence spectroscopy.

The MS having wt% compositions: Sn, 0.01 %; Cu, 0.01 %; Si, 0.02; P, 0.02 %; Ni,0.02 %; Mn, 0.11 %; C, 0.12 % and rest was Fe, was used as metal substrate. For the weight loss studies, the MS sheet was cut into 3.0 × 3.5 × 0.5 cm rectangular specimens. While, the square shaped MS working electrode having 1 cm2 exposed area was used for the electrochemical measurements. Before each measurement, the MS specimens and electrodes were mechanically polished with emery papers (grit size 80 to 4000). Polished specimens were washed with distilled water followed by acetone and dried with a cold air blower.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of 15 % HCl solution and inhibitor solution
ce:section.ce:para: Analytical grade commercially available 37 % HCl, was diluted to prepare 15 % HCl solution. Whereas, for the inhibitor solution, 1 g powder of inhibitor was taken in a volumetric flask and dissolved in 1000 mL of the prepared 15 % HCl solution (Stock inhibitor solution). For desired concentration, the stock solution was diluted.

The corrosion inhibition studies were performed by using weight loss method, polarization method and EIS measurements Inhibitor solution was changed after each reading. For the weight loss study the MS specimens were immersed in 15 % HCl solution without and with different concentration of inhibitor for 6 h at different temperatures (303, 313, 323 and 333 K). Each experiment was repeated three times for more accuracy. While for the electrochemical study the experiment was performed at 303 K.

The surface study of uninhibited and inhibited specimens was done by using FESEM and AFM analysis. The composition of the protective layer formed at the MS specimen surface was done by using XPS analysis.

For the preparation of specimens for FESEM analysis, a set of four polished MS tokens were taken, three out of four were dipped separately in (i) 15 % HCl solution without inhibitor (ii) 15 % HCl solution with optimum concentration of CD1, and (iii) 15 % HCl solution with optimum concentration of CD2, for 6 h at 303 K temperature. All three token were removed from solution, cleaned and dried. These three specimens and one neat polished specimen of MS were scanned by FESEM supra 55 (Carl Zeiss Germany) instrument accessible in CRF of IIT-ISM, Dhanbad.

The specimens for AFM analysis were prepared in the same way as in case of FESEM study and AFM images were taken by using Dimension Icon and Multimode 8 (Bruker Corporation) instrument available in CRF of IIT-ISM, Dhanbad.

The XPS was used for investigating the composition of synthesized CDs and the inhibitor-metal complex on the MS specimen surface. Separate XPS spectra were taken for the CDS as well as MS samples that were immersed in 15 HCl solution with the optimum dosage of CD1 and CD2 inhibitors for 6 h immersion. The obtained spectra were deconvoluted in Origin 8.5 to know and identify elemental species in various oxidation states and chemical environments on the surface. The versa probe III (PHI's 5000 versa probe III) was used to scan the sample surface installed in the CRF of IIT-ISM, Dhanbad.

FTIR spectra of CD1 (Fig. 2 a) has a broad band at 3434 cm−1 assigned for –OH & -NH groups [30]. The stretching vibration at 1683 cm−1 is due to CO /C=N group [31]. The peaks situated at 1400 cm−1 and 1110 cm−1 are assigned for stretching vibrations of CO/CN groups [32,33]. While in case of CD2 (Fig. 2b), the broad band at 3411 cm−1 is recognized to –OH & -NH groups [34]. The peak situated at 1703 cm−1 is produced by stretching of CO group. The vibration peak at 1656 cm−1 is assigned to CN group [35]. The characteristic peaks at 1407 cm−1 and 1112 cm−1 are produced by stretching vibrations of CO and CN groups [32,33].

Hydrothermally synthesized CD1 and CD2 have blue colour florescence in UV-light. So, their optical properties were examined by UV–vis and PL study. The CD1 revealed UV absorbance peak at 395 nm (Fig. 2c) assigned to the n-π⁎ transition of C=N/C=O. The PL image shown in Fig. 2e has maximum emission excitation wavelength at 395 nm. On the other hand CD2 revealed UV adsorption peaks at 323 nm and 326 nm (Fig. 2d) assign to the n- π⁎ transition of C=N/C=O [33]. Furthermore, the PL image shown in Fig. 2f has maximum emission excitation wavelength at 350 nm.

The size and morphology of CD1 and CD2 were examined by HRTEM and presented in Fig. 3 . The CD1 and CD2 had asymmetric sphere-shaped morphology. The average sizes of CD1 and CD2 were recorded to be 4.5 and 3.4 nm, respectively. The HRTEM micrographs with EDX, as present in Fig. 3, confirmed the successful synthesis of CD1 and CD2.

XPS study was performed to recognise the composition of CD1 and CD2. The surface survey shown in Fig. 4 represents the existence of C, O, and N in CD1 & CD2 and atomic weight percent of C, N, and O in CD1 and CD2 is shown in Table 1 . Furthermore, deconvoluted spectra of C, O, and N are given in Fig. 4.

In CD1 deconvoluted spectra, three kind of carbon peaks (Fig. 4) assigned as CC/CH (284.2) [36], CN/CN (285.4) [37,38], and CO (287.9) [38] were observed. Deconvoluted CD2 spectra (Fig. 4) also exhibited three peaks assigned to CC/CH (283.9) [30], CN/CN (284.9) [31,32], and CO (287.5) [38]. Similarly, O 1s deconvoluted spectra of CD1 and CD2 revealed two kind of oxygen bonding corresponding to CO (530.4) and CO (531.4) for CD1 and CO (529.8) and CO (531.1) for CD2 [39,40]. Furthermore deconvoluted spectra of N 1s revealed the three peaks corresponding to NC (398.6), NC (399.3), and NH (400.4) for CD1 and NC (399.1), NC (399.7), and NH (400.3) for CD2 [41]. The outcomes of XPS studies confirm the synthesis of expected compounds CD1 and CD2.

The anticorrosion test for inhibitors CD1 and CD2 was done via two different methods and tried to correlate the outcomes of these two studies. The weight-loss method is a conventional method. In contrast, the other method is electrochemical method which is highly reliable.

Weight loss method is regarded as an easy, genuine and inexpensive experimental technique for demonstrating the metallic corrosive actions and the inhibition proficiency of a corrosion inhibitor for the metal. The rate of corrosion (
C R

ce:section.ce:section-title: The impact of concentration and temperature on corrosion inhibition
ce:section.ce:para: Table 2 displays that the C R of MS significantly decreases as the dosage of inhibitor increases in 15 % HCl solution, suggesting that addition of corrosion inhibitors CD1 and CD2 reduce the rate of corrosion of the mild steel. The variation of C R with change in concentration of CD1 & CD2 is depicted in Fig. 5 (a, c). The C R for the MS specimen exposed to 15 % HCl was 32.4 mmy−1 at 303 K, that increasing significantly to 160.5 mmy−1 at 333 K. The lowest observed C R value for CD1 and CD2 at 200 ppm dosage and 303 K temperature were 1.0 mmy−1 and 1.7 mmy−1, respectively. The significant fall in the values of C R were also observed at elevated temperatures. Significant reduction in C R with rise in concentration of CD1 & CD2 is recognized due to the fabrication of a defensive inhibitor layer on the MS substrate.

A careful investigation of Table 2 & Fig. 5 (b, d) indicate that the corrosion inhibition proficiency (η%) increases with concentration up to 150 ppm concentration after that almost constant value is observed. So, 150 ppm dosage is considered as the optimum concentration for the CD1 and CD2. The η% value for CD1 and CD2 at 25 ppm concentration and 303 K temperature are 76.2 % & 73.9 %, respectively. For the CD1 and CD2 inhibitors, the η% values increased with increasing amount of inhibitor. At 150 ppm concentration of CD1 and CD2, the η% was observed as 96.8 % and 94.4, respectively. After 150 ppm concentration only slight incensement in η% values are noticed. This investigation reveals that the inhibitors seal the most of active corrosion sites at 150 ppm, so with further addition of inhibitors only a slight increment is observed [43]. The inhibitor CD1 contains arginine and CD2 has histidine along with other components which are common in both the inhibitors. Therefore higher corrosion inhibition efficiency offered by CD1 as compared to CD2 is due to presence of more number of electron donating nitrogen atoms, larger size and planner structure of arginine present in CD1 as compared to histidine present in CD2. More number of nitrogen atoms, larger size and planner structure of arginine present in CD1 causes strong adsorption of CD1 as compared to CD2 on mild steel surface and offers better efficiency as compared to CD2. Thus CD1 acts as better inhibitor than CD2. The corrosion inhibition efficiency of studied inhibitors CD1 and CD2 is better than most of the similar type of corrosion inhibitors reported in literature.

The weight loss study data was used to analyse the activation and thermodynamic parameters for concentrations ranging from 25 ppm to 150 ppm. This concentration range was chosen because 150 ppm was determined to be the optimum concentration of CD1 and CD2.

Arrhenius (Eq. (4)) and transition state (Eq. (5)) equations [44] were plotted to calculate activation energy (
E a

R vs 1000/T (Arrhenius plot (Fig. 6 ). Then, the slope of each plot is fit in the

The
E a
values of the inhibitors CD1 and CD2 are found to increase with increase in dosage and are found to be higher compared to 15 % HCl electrolyte. This observation suggests the hindrance in rate of corrosion reaction. The higher
E a
values of CD1 inhibitor compared to CD2 at all concentrations indicate that molecules of CD1 have greater corrosion inhibition extent. The magnitude of desorption of the molecules of water increases as the temperature rises, leaving a greater surface area vacant for the adsorption of the CD1 and CD2 molecules. Similar to Arrhenius plots, the liner plots of log C
R
/T vs 1000/T (Fig. 6) were used to determine the magnitude of

and

ΔS ∗

ΔS ∗
reveal the molecules of CD1 and CD2 inhibitors associated to the surface of MS tokens in the rate-determining stage. And the magnitude of
ΔS ∗
were found to rise with increasing dosage of CD1 and CD2. This analysis is attributed to water molecule desorption from the MS in the presence of CD1 and CD2 molecules. As a result, the molecules of CD1 and CD2 eliminate previously adsorbed molecules of water from the MS while forming a defensive layer. The + ve value of
ΔH ∗
suggests the endothermic nature of the substrate dissolution in 15 % hydrochloric acid electrolyte. The unaffected positive values with various dosage of the inhibitors implies that the CD1 and CD2 don't modify the kind of the corrosion procedure while giving fortification against the aggressive 15 % HCl electrolyte [44,45].

Mitigation of corrosion by inhibitors CD1 and CD2 occurs through forming a protective film upon the surface of MS tokens. The molecules of inhibitors adsorb on the tokens of the MS and mechanically protect it from corrosion caused by the 15 % HCl electrolyte. The study of the adsorption isotherms revealed useful evidences about the nature of interaction of the CD1 and CD2 molecules with the MS substrate. According to the literature, usually the effective organic inhibitors (OCIs) adsorb on the surface of substrate and simultaneously suppress the rates of anodic substrate dissolution reactions and cathodic hydrogen evaluation. Langmuir, Frumkin, and Temkin isotherms were tested and Langmuir adsorption isotherm was found to be best fit among these adsorption isotherm with the R 2 closest to unity. The Langmuir equation is shown as Eq. (6) and Langmuir plot is represented in Fig. 7 .
(6)

is the dosages of CD1 and CD2,
K ads
is the adsorption-desorption equilibrium constant, and θ is the coverage area of tokens by inhibitors.

The intercepts of the Fig. 7 was used to estimate the magnitude of
K ads
. Whereas, the magnitude of free energy of adsorption (
ΔG ads
) was computed by putting the value of
K ads
in Eq. (7) and values are represented in Table 4 .
(7)

1000
K ads
where, R is equal to 8.314 J K−1 mol−1, T is temperatures of experiments, and 1000 is concentration of H2O in gL−1.

Large
K ads
values were obtained for both CD1 and CD2 inhibitors, indicating that both inhibitors have strong adsorption and high corrosion inhibition proficiency. The –ve
ΔG ads
values of both inhibitors suggest spontaneous adsorption of CD1 and CD2 molecules on the MS surface [46]. Furthermore, the
ΔG ads
values were found to be within the range of −29.47 to −30.73 kJ mol−1 for CD1 and −29.12 to −29.67 kJ mol−1 for CD2 in the temperature range 303–333 K. It has been reported that if the
ΔG ads
values are less negative than −20 kJ mole−1, the nature of adsorption is physical, whereas if it is more negative than −40 kJ mole−1, the nature of adsorption is chemical adsorption. If the values are between −20 and −40 kJ mole−1, it indicates physicochemical adsorption [47].

So in order to confirm the kind of adsorption, the magnitudes of enthalpy of adsorption (
ΔH ads o
) and entropy of adsorption (
ΔS ads o
) were computed using Eq. (8) [48].
(8)
ΔG ads o
=
ΔH ads o

TΔS ads o

The plot of
ΔG ads o
vs. Temperature (Fig. 7) is linear, with the slope indicating -
ΔS ads o
and the intercept indicating
ΔH ads o
. The calculated values of
ΔH ads o
and
ΔS ads o
are shown in Table 4. It has been reported that chemisorption occurs when the calculated magnitude of
ΔH ads o
is greater than −100 kJmol−1, whereas physisorption occurs when the calculated value is less than or around −40 kJmol−1. The endothermic inhibitor adsorption is represented by positive
ΔH ads o
values, whereas exothermic adsorption is represented by negative
ΔH ads o
values [48]. Here, the calculated values of
ΔH ads o
for CD1 and CD2 are −15.98 kJmol−1 and -23.71 kJmol−1, respectively. This observation demonstrates that the adsorption is physisorption and exothermic. The negative
ΔS ads o
value of inhibitors suggests inhibitor adsorption on the MS surface. The entropy of the MS-15%HCl interface is initially high due to the freely motion of CD1 and CD2 molecules in the 15%HCl. As the inhibitors molecules bind to the MS substrate, the MS-15%HCl interfacial system tends a lower entropy state. Therefore, it is clear that the adsorption process of the investigated CD1 and CD2 molecules is primarily physical in nature.

System stability is very important in electrochemical corrosion experiments. Therefore, before running electrochemical experiments, the reactions at the working electrode and solution interface must reached to a steady state [49,50]. Accordingly, the variation of potential of MS electrode vs immersion times at various concentration of CD1 and CD2 in 15 % HCl solution was measured as shown in Fig. 8 a, b. It is clear from Fig. 8 a, b that after about 500 s, all the samples achieved the steady state condition (OCP).

it can be concluded from Fig. 8 a, b that OCP of both the inhibitors shifted toward positive potential with respect to the bare 15 % HCl solution and difference is within ±85 mV. Therefore both the inhibitors act as mixed type inhibitor and dominant in anodic character. It CD1 and CD2 inhibitors adsorbed on both the cathodic and anodic sites simultaneously to suppress the rates of anodic MS dissolution reaction and cathodic hydrogen evaluation reaction [51,52].

The electrochemical kinetics of MS electrode dipped in 15 % hydrochloric acid at 303 K temperature, without and with various concentration of inhibitor was examined by PP study. As a result, the typical PP curves of MS in 15 % HCl corrosive medium having different dosage of CD1 & CD2 are presented in Fig. 8 c, d. Some significant polarization parameters, such as corrosion current density (
i corr
), corrosion potential (
E corr
), cathodic polarization slopes (
β c
), anodic polarization slopes (
β a
), and corrosion inhibition proficiency ( %
η tf
), were derived from these curves to obtain evidence regarding the kinetics of corrosion, and are tabled in Table 5 . The values of

&

were directly found by the extrapolation of the polarization curve to the corresponding corrosion potentials through the Corrtest instrument via auto fit Tafel region. Whereas, the value of corrosion inhibition efficiency ( %

) were calculated by the Eq. (9) [53].

The magnitudes of
i corr
reduced, as increasing the concentration of CD1 & CD2 inhibitors. This observation supports the fabrication of a shielding film on the working MS electrode -HCl interface, resulting in a rise in corrosion inhibition proficiency ( %
η tf
) and a decrease in the C R [54]. The polarization curves reveal that the inhibitors influence both the anodic and cathodic slopes (
β c
&
β a
), as a result, the reactions on both anodic and cathodic sites are suppressed. The nature of polarization curves remain almost same in absence and presence of inhibitors suggesting that corrosion mechanism of MS is not changing by addition of inhibitors in 15 % HCL solution. The mixed nature of both inhibitors supports that presence inhibitor decreases the rate anodic dissolution of MS and cathodic hydrogen evolution reaction implying that CD1 and CD2 strongly inhibits the corrosion of mild steel. Corrosion inhibition efficiency of both inhibitors increases on increasing the concentration of both inhibitors due to adsorption of inhibitor molecules on anodic and cathodic sites of mild steel.

The minor difference in corrosion potential (
E corr
) of MS in bare 15 % HCl solution and solution containing CD1 & CD2 inhibitor indicates that CD1 and CD2 act as mixed type inhibitor. The
E corr
values in presence of both inhibitors detected to shift slightly toward the anodic region suggests that CD1 & CD2 as mixed inhibitor and dominant toward anodic behavior. The deviation of
E corr
is well within ±85 mV, thus it can be concluded that CD1 & CD2 serve as mixed type corrosion inhibitors [55].

EIS examinations were executed to know the impedance parameters of MS electrode in 15 % HCl without and with various concentrations of CD1& CD2. The Nyquist curves obtained from the experiment are presented in Fig. 9 a, b and impedance parameters are summarized in Table 6 . Fig. 9 a, b, represents a single capacitive depressed semicircle suggesting presence of onetime constant. The diameter of the semicircle increases with CD1 & CD2 concentration, suggesting charge transfer resistance (R
ct ) increases with CD1 & CD2 concentration, despite the fact that their appearance remained unchanged. The increase in R
ct becomes more pronounced as the CD1 & CD2 concentrations increased, proposing that CD1 & CD2 molecules adsorb on the MS electrode. As shown in Fig. 9a and b, the Nyquist curves are depressed semicircle inhomogeneities of the surface of MS electrode [56].

The Nyquist capacitive circles were fitted against the suitable electrical circuit shown in Fig. 9 e to acquire magnitudes of parameters like R
ct , and constant phase elements (CPE)
Y o
and exponent n (0 < n < 1) in the absence and presence of CD1 & CD2 inhibitor. The electrical circuit cosist of solution resistance (
R s
) and charge transfer resistance (
R ct
), which are connected in parallel with the CPE. Table 6 summarises the corrosion parameters obtained by EIS measurements.

The corrosion inhibition efficiencies ( %
E i
) and double layer capacitance (
C dl
) of CD1& CD2 inhibitors were calculated from charge transfer value and
Y o
value by using Eqs. (10) & (11) [57].
(10)
%
E i
=
R ct i

R ct 0

Table 6 reveals that after the addition of the inhibitor, magnitudes of C dl diminished and the magnitudes of
R ct
increased. The decrease in C dl is due to decrease in local dielectric constant and/or an increase in the thickness of the double layer [58]. Adsorption of larger CD1 and CD2 molecules reduces capacitance by increasing the double layer thickness. The magnitudes of
R ct
increses with CD1 & CD2 concentration due to adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the electrode surface. The inhibitor CD1 and CD2 offered inhibition efficiency of 96.5 and 94.5 % respectively.

Bode plots for MS electrodes in absence and presence of CD1 and CD2 in 15 % HCl solution at 303 K are shown schematically in Fig. 9 c, d. On careful inspection, the magnitudes of phase angle (θ) and log |Z| of the blank solutions are zero in the high-frequency region, corresponding to solution resistance around the mild steel electrode and reference electrode. In general, the slope and phase angle (θ) of an ideal capacitor are −1 and 90o, respectively. The variation from ideal capacitor is due to roughness of the MS electrode in an aggressive acid medium. The broad shape at maximum in the intermediate frequencies region indicate that the inhibitors adsorb and form a thin protective layer at the MS-15%HCl interface. The maximum magnitudes at intermediate frequencies displays only one maximum, implying that only one constant is noticed and thus only one relaxation process has occurred during the charge transfer process [59]. Further, the increase in absolute impedance at low frequencies in the Bode plot confirms that the concentration of inhibitors increases protection. The outcomes of EIS measurement agree perfectly with the outcomes of weight-loss and potentiodynamic studies.

Fig. 10 depicts the surface marphology of MS specimen without and with 150 ppm inhibitor. Micrograph 10 a, represents the surface of polished specimen which looks uniform and smooth. While micrograph 10b reveals the surface of MS specimen that immersed in 15 % HCl without inhibitor. A significant change in the morphology of micrograph 10 b suggests high degree of corrosion in aggressive 15 % HCl without inhibitor. Similarly, micrographs 10 c and 11d represent the surface of MS specimen submerged in 15 % HCl with 150 ppm CD1 and CD2. The less defection is observed in its morphology compare to micrograph 10 b. It indicates the adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the MS specimen. Thus, this observation supported fabrication of a protective film on the MS surface by CD1 & CD2 molecules. Furthermore, the lower rough surface of micrograph 10(c) as compared to 11d recommend that CD1 is a better inhibitor than CD2.

AFM analysis is a powerful technique to detect topology of any surface from micro to nano scale. So the scale of corrosion on the MS specimen was detected by AFM instrument. Fig. 11 represents the 3D-micrographs of four MS specimens whereas their roughness values are tabled in Table 7 . The observed roughness values of specimens were 2106, 431, 234, and 35.9 nm for 15 % HCl without inhibitor, with 150 ppm CD2, with 150 ppm CD1, and for the polished specimen, respectively. The significant reduction in roughness value for the specimens immersed with inhibitors suggests corrosion was inhibited by inhibitors. The obtained data supported the hypothesis that the smoothness of the MS surface is due to the fabrication of a packed protective layer on the surface of MS by CD1 & CD2. The lower roughness value for CD1 than CD2 suggested that CD1 is a better inhibitor than CD2.

XPS study was executed to confirm the fabrication of the protective film of inhibitor molecules on MS surface and to investigate the composition of the film made on the MS sample. The survey spectra of CD1 and CD2 are present in Fig. 12 , which reveal the occurrence of C, O, N and Fe. So, the survey scan agrees the existence of C, N, and O elements which were present in the inhibitors, and their atomic % is tabled in Table 8 .

Deconvoluted C, N, O, and Fe spectra of CD1 + Fe is shown in Fig. 12. The C 1s deconvoluted peaks were recorded with the binding energies (BEs) at 284.1, 284.2, 285.3, and 287.7 eV related to CFe, CC/CH, CN/CN, and CO bonding, respectively [36–38,60]. Similarly, the raw spectrum of N 1s was deconvoluted into three peaks at BEs 398.1, 399.4, and 400.5 eV for the bond types NC, NC, NH/NFe, respectively [41,61,62]. The deconvoluted O 1s four peaks at BEs 529.2, 530.8, 531.4, and 532.5 eV were related to FeO, CO, CO, and hydroxyl groups [39,40,62]. The Fe 2p was deconvoluted into five peaks. The first four peak of Fe 2p3/2 were assigned as Fe0 (708.4 eV), Fe2+(709.9 eV& 711.0), and FeCl3 (713.8 eV) and the next two peaks at 722.7 and 728.2 eV arises due to Fe 2p1/2 [63–65].

Deconvoluted C, N, O, and Fe spectra of CD2 + Fe is shown in Fig. 13 . The C 1s deconvoluted peaks were recorded with the BEs at 283.6, 284.1, 284.9, and 287.1 eV related to CFe, CC/CH, CN/CN, and CO bonding respectively [36–38,60]. Similarly, the raw spectrum of N 1s was deconvoluted into three peaks at BEs 398.7, 399.4, and 400.5 for the bond type NC, NC, NH/NFe [41,61,62]. The deconvoluted O 1s four peaks at BEs 529.7, 530.2, and 531.5 eV were related to FeO, CO, and CO [39,40,62]. The Fe 2p peak is deconvoluted into five peaks. The first three peak of Fe 2p3/2 were assigned as Fe0 (709.1 eV), Fe2+(710.2 eV) and FeCl3 (714.4 eV) and the next two peaks at 722.5, and 728.2 eV arises due to Fe 2p1/2 [63–65].

The outcomes of XPS study suggested that the existence of CFe, FeO and FeN bonds, suggesting the development of CD1 and CD2 film on the MS specimen. These inhibitor films shield the MS from the aggressive corrosive medium and suppress the rate of corrosion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of examined CD1 and CD2 inhibitors with previously reported CDs inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: Table 9 compares the inhibition efficiency of some previously reported CDs on the various type of MS substrate in hydrochloric acid corrosive medium [66–79] with CD1 and CD2 inhibitors investigated in the present work. Table 9 suggests that the CD1 is more effective than the majority of the reported CDs inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of corrosion inhibitors CD1& CD2
ce:section.ce:para: The molecules of inhibitors CD1 & CD2 exist either as neutral or in the protonated (cationic) form. The adsorption of neutral CD1 & CD2 inhibitor molecules on the MS/HCl interface obey chemisorption mechanism via involving sharing of electrons between functional group of heteroatoms and iron d-orbitals of the mild steel. The other possibility of donor-acceptor interaction can occur between π-electrons of CD1& CD2 molecules and vacant d orbital of iron surface. The protonated molecules of CD1 & CD2 inhibitor followed physical adsorption via electrostatic interactions between positively charged CD1 and CD2 and chloride ion already adsorbed on the MS surface. In the current study, the kind of adsorption is primarily physical in nature. It means the functional groups of CD1 & CD2 inhibitor molecules exist in protonated phase in HCl medium.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Carbon dots CD1 & CD2 were hydrothermally synthesized and characterized by using HRTEM, FTIR, UV-spectroscopy, photoluminescence, and XPS.
Carbon dots CD1 & CD2 act as good corrosion inhibitor for mild steel in 15 % HCl solution.
The Carbon dots CD1 & CD2 offers corrosion inhibition efficiency of 96.8 % and 94.7 %, respectively, at 303 K and 150 ppm concentration.
The inhibitor CD1 is better inhibitor than CD2 due to presence of more active sites and planner structure.
The adsorption of CD1 & CD2 on mild steel surface obeyed Langmuir adsorption isotherm.
Potentiodynamic polarization studies showed that CD1 & CD2 act as mixed inhibitor.
The FESEM, AFM and XPS studies suggested the adsorption of CD1 & CD2 on mild steel surface.
The results of corrosion inhibition efficiency obtained by weight loss method, polarization studies and EIS methods are in good agreement with each other.
